Hi, welcome back to the celebrity branding blueprint. My name is Greg Roulette and joining me again here in this module is my partner, Emmy Award-winning director and bestselling author Nick Nanton. And here in this module, we are now into stage three because we've developed a niche, we've developed our story, which really is our brand. Now we want to elevate it to this celebrity expert status. So let's start by talking about what this celebrity expert status is all about and why it's really important to those watching here in this module. Yeah, absolutely. Becoming a celebrity expert allows you freedom really. Dan Sullivan, another great entrepreneurial coach, talks about the four freedoms entrepreneurs are really after. Yeah. After freedom of time, freedom of money, freedom of relationships, and freedom of place. And celebrity branding allows you to have those on whatever level you desire. Now I'm not going to go to the Tim Ferriss land and say, you don't have to work four hours a week. And I mean, there's some amount of money you could make working only four hours a week. But then there's a lot of problems with that just in general with continuity of trying to pick back up next week and figure out where you left off. And I couldn't do it. No, not my desire of a great life, but the celebrity branding will allow you and becoming a celebrity expert will allow you to have the freedom. It's more like autonomy. Yes. Just along with freedom is because you get to select the clients that you want to work with because we've hand selected our niche. We're doing this direct marketing. We're creating the story and we're attracting the people that we want to work with, which ultimately leads to a better life because we're not taking the riffraff anything that comes in the door. We're only taking those that we can really help and benefit, right? Absolutely. And that is the key. So, you have to set it up so that you attract the people who you can work with and you repel the people you can't. It's been said that the strength at which you repel people is also the strength at which you attract people. I love that. Not everyone's going to be a good fit. It doesn't mean you have to be rude about it. But the more, and we talked a little bit in the last module about putting things out there, I mean, I know a lot of people who put their political beliefs in everything, their marriage beliefs, all that stuff. And again, the strength at which you repel is the strength at which you attract. So don't be afraid to try some things. Again, it doesn't mean you have to be rude or confrontational about it, but don't be afraid to be who you are because that's all you can be. Exactly. So, I really think that a true celebrity, whether you're a mainstream Hollywood celebrity or if you're kind of on this, you know, being the local celebrity, the niche celebrity that we want to get to, it really starts with first impressions, right? So Hollywood celebrities, you know one when they walk into a room. There's an aura around them. There's, you know, people are just like, who is that? They know that they're somebody just by how they walk in the room. And for a celebrity expert, the same thing is true. And obviously there's a lot of entry points, right? Someone could come to your website first. They could see you at an event. They could get your book at a bookstore or online. There's a lot of these different entry points, but it's all based on first impressions. And they need to know from the instant that they make that first impression with you that you are the guy, right? Absolutely. So, I mean, when we have people into our experiences, some of them include photo shoots. Yeah. And some of the things that people, they kind of don't understand the value of it. Look, we've got a thing running behind us right now with our photos on them. And that was me before the biggest loser. So I need to, I got to get a new one there. But no, but the fact of the matter is that, look, when people, when people see your photo, that's one of the first opportunities you have to build, to start building a relationship. Right. Again, we talked about repetition before. We talked about creating this relationship. The beauty of what we're going to teach here is that you can take one to many forms of communication and build one-to-one relationships. We talked about the newsletter story before, people were coming up to me, talking to me. I was communicating one to many. They felt like it was one-to-one, so it opened up the door. So one of the first things you can do by adding photos to your stuff, to your business card, to your website, to your book covers, it's not because we think we're great looking people. We might be, but... We might be. We'll be okay if you tell us we were. But we understand the fact that adding a great looking brand photo to everything we do allows people to connect with you. And then when you meet them, coming up shortly, we have a welcome reception for clients, a bunch of them we've never met before. They'll walk in the door and they'll go, hey, Nick, hey, Greg, they know all about us. I've seen the videos of... So presenting a very good first impression is crucial. Now, what does that look like? Well, for different people, it's different. I have this look of a sports coat and jeans. It works well for an entertainment lawyer or a guy who's got entertainment and agent. You kind of have this button-up Madison Avenue thing, which is cool. It's like Texas 100 degrees outside. So it's perfect. But it fits your story. So you've got to figure out who am I trying to beat to who and how can I fit that? Not that you can't break some of the rules, particularly if it makes sense to break the rules. Again, I'm a lawyer, but I wear this when there's people decked out to the nines and suits. And I get away with it because I have the story to make sense. But first impressions are huge. And one of the best ways to make a great first impression is with a photo that is approachable, professional, but has some polish to it. And I'll emphasize approachable. When I was in music business, every band wants to look hard, whatever. And it's like, man. The posse pose. I get it. And maybe that works in music. I'm not even convinced it does. But a smile is so inviting. A smile is huge. People are already intimidated enough of how much you probably know. Don't give a reason not to call you. You want to look friendly, inviting, like the person they really want to become friends with. Yeah. And so a couple of things to note just in our own marketing, because now if you're going through this product, you've been engulfed in us in some way, that our photos are literally on everything. So you see it on the backdrop that's behind me right now. But you see it on our newsletter. On our newsletter, Envelope has the photos on it. Every postcard, every sales letter, videos is all of us personally. We don't hide behind a white PowerPoint slide or anything. And so, but again, it creates this familiarity where people, they know us. Even if I've never met them, even if they've been on only, they've seen our podcasts or something. They feel like they know who I am. And it goes into this concept that you teach, and I'm going to butcher what you call it, but it's where you start to take up a spot that might be consumed by your grandma. But now we're taking it up because you feel like we have a real one-to-one connection. So why don't you teach a little bit about how we can start taking up mental capacity of our marketplace. Sure. So there's this rule called done-borrow, right? Yeah. And it's kind of done-borrowed in this research. And he determined that you can really only have relationships with about 100 people at any one particular time. Now I'm not saying number one being your spouse or best friend or something, and number 100 are the same. But that's about what your brain can handle. And also the US military did research at the same time and determined about the same thing, about the same, the maximum people they could put effectively into, I don't know, military terms very well, but a platoon or a company or whatever it is, was like 100 people, right? And so that's the number you need to know. Now here's the thing. When you use media effectively and you show up in someone's mailbox a lot, you show up in their email box a lot, you show up in their videos a lot, you start to take over one of those 100 relationships. For instance, the effect of media on that is if someone watches Oprah every day, Oprah takes one of those spots. Again, I'm not saying that you can't distinguish between, I'm not saying you can't distinguish between Oprah and your girlfriends that you go with. But the fact of the matter is that they are in those 100 relationships. So as you show up more with your photo, your video, your messaging in someone's life, you become the Oprah by force really. You start showing up. You start taking one of those slots, the more they read, the more they consume, the more they, the higher up that ladder you go. What happens is essentially those 100 people are friends, right? And so if you're a friend and you're in a certain business, you become their friend in the business. If you have a friend who's in the car business and you need something done to your car, you probably don't call around for three quotes. You probably don't question the price they give you. Just a friend in the business, you know they're going to take care of you. You're going to get well taken care of. And that's kind of it. And so we get rid of all of these issues with price, with deliverability, with trust, all this stuff just by showing up a lot, by using media and by looking trusting, right? Yeah, exactly. So kind of taking that to the next level is so now you're indoctrinating, you're ingraining your marketplace with you, right, in your personality. The second half of it goes back to the secret formula for me to success because now there needs to be some credibility and some reputable things about you out there. And so that's being seen on ABC, NBC, CBS and Fox, having a book and a best selling book at that. It's being seen in the publications where your marketplace wants you to be seen. I mean, you know, if I'm in personal training, it might not do me any good to be in a dental magazine or something, but it means a whole lot to be in men's fitness or men's health. So let's talk about now putting this together because now we have, you know, our photos, our story, our brand, we're going out to our niche. Now we need third-party credibility, right? You got to have third-party credibility for all the reasons we've already discussed, but the fact that to make people pay attention to the fact that you are who you say you are and also position use one of the best in the marketplace. I love the story. My wife was looking for a doctor for something a few years ago. I don't remember what it was, but she called me and said, hey, honey, I finally found a doctor. I was like, oh, great. I finally found it. Did someone refer you to them? Or she goes, no, I Googled and found this guy and it says right his website. He's been on NBC. So yeah, I'm like, who is it? Tell me who it is. It might be my client. It wasn't my client, but the point was even my wife who knows that my job is to help people get this kind of credibility. It works on her. It's credibility to her because it is credibility because at the end of the day, we all know that the guy who's been on TV has some extra special knowledge. He's got a little extra special fairy dust. He's got whatever it is. That person has something unique that other people aren't getting on TV. Yeah. Now there's also a ladder of credibility that we talk a lot about and that's one of the concepts I want to cover here because I think it's really important to note that just because you've been on TV once eight years ago, you got to keep stacking things on top of each other. You got to continue to do this stuff. So let's talk about what the ladder of credibility is and how do we climb that ladder and what happens when we start getting rungs up into that ladder? Sure. So in every field, there's kind of a baseline. In professional fields where it requires licensing like financial advisor, like most financial advisors have to have series seven or whatever those licenses are. In law, I had to pass the bar. As a dentist, you have to pass, I think it's your boards, whatever. There's like, that's the baseline. So real estate agent, you have to have a license. So there's some baseline and so a lot of people think- But the thing about the baseline is everyone has to have that. So some lawyers are like, well, I'm a member of the bar. Well, so is every other lawyer. So that's where you start. Now what the ladder of credibility is is adding other things that give you additional credibility. So again, I'll use me for instance, and we can do the same for you because you've done the same. But from there, I wrote a bestselling book. I wrote another bestselling book. So each thing you do that adds credibility, gives you another rum on the ladder. So I've been at NBC, ABC, CBS, Fox, One Emmys, all this stuff, and you're just stacking up the ladder of credibility. And what happens is as you add more and more and more, you get more rums on the ladder. And as I say, you eventually are towering over all of your competition and when any of your prospects looks up, all they see is you. Someone else might be hanging here, here, here, and you're just towering above them because you have so much credibility. And that's where the ladder of credibility is. It is using everything you've got. You have to use it. I know you've got it. But using it to add those rums to the ladder because ultimately then you become the only choice because you're the only one they can see. You're everywhere they're looking. We talk a lot about people want to get noticed. They want to be seen. They want to get out there. They want to get some exposure. Well, really the only exposure of being seen you want or need or want to afford is in the places that your target market is looking. The people who hire us, they're like, Nick, I can't, I feel like, I hadn't even heard of you six months ago or whatever. But now everywhere I go, you're all, I see you everywhere. How do you do this? Well, I'm not everywhere everyone else looks. You're a target client. So I'm going to make sure I'm dominating the media places that you're looking, whether it's your, as a lawyer, the bar journal and whether, you know, speaking at the bar conference, I'm speaking at this marketing conference for lawyers and whatever it is, I'm starting to show up where you are. And that's key. Right. So, really, everywhere that they are looking, that's a super key phrase, is that you do want to be everywhere they're looking. What are the blogs that they read? What are the newsletters that they get? What are the trade journals, the magazines? And then getting to them in the inbox, obviously, through email, getting them into the mailbox. You know, crazy idea there. But we're getting into the mailbox. So literally everywhere they turn around, they saw a video from me today. They had a friend promoting you on their site. They go to their mailbox and they pull out a package from you. You are literally everywhere. And that's how you use Dunbar's law, really, because you literally everywhere they turn, Nick is everywhere. Eventually, you're going to be a big poster of Nick on the wall. Right. It's like you're a friend. You're seeing him. You're making a phone. You're going to hang out with him. You're going to have lunch with him. You're having drinks with him, whatever it is. And so I'm doing the same thing. And you can too, just by using media and showing up. I think it's a good point too now to talk about physical versus digital newsletters is a great way to start. Definitely. So, again, everyone has email newsletters and not a bad thing to have at all. We have one. They're great. They are inexpensive. Right. And you set them out. Most of us get some number of hundreds of emails a day. I'm just piling up here as we go. Right. Yeah. We have hundreds of emails a day and most of them are asking for some level of interaction. Hey, Nick, what time do I need to be there? Or what's the thing? Or, you know, hey, are you coming to school with your son, Brock, next week? Whatever it is. Yeah. They're asking for a response. I consider it rude not to respond to those or at least to make sure someone else responds to them. The rest of it is like email lists were a part of Amazon, Flyers, all that stuff. And on the list, like I'm super hot. On one particular topic right now, then I'm probably just going to delete all those Flyers. Right. Unless again, it's a big affinity that I've got. And then I'm going to respond to the personal correspondence. And that's kind of my day's worth of communication, right? And so what happens is as an email newsletter, you're probably in the bottom of that, you know, there's personal correspondence and then there's everything else. In the email box, you're kind of at the bottom of that bucket. You really are. So you get deleted. In the mailbox, again, we said earlier that Dan Kennedy says a good newsletter should feel like the equivalent of sitting down and have a cup of coffee with a good friend. Well, what that does is it creates a relationship and how many people watching this are likely to be getting regular correspondence from friends in the mail. I mean, how much regular correspondence do you get from friends? I mean, my son gets birthday cards. Birthday cards or birthday invitations. Maybe some balance. Yeah. But that's about it. Christmas cards maybe. Right. You're not getting letters in the mail. Right. I'm not writing you letters in the mail. I like you at all. Yeah, right. It's not happening, right? I'm writing a newsletter that feels like sitting down and having a cup of coffee with a good friend, with a Nick and Greg. Right. Then that now rises to the top of the mailbox. So everything else is bills and garbage. So now the same exact content can be in the bottom of the email box or the top of the mailbox. I'm going to go to the top of the mailbox so I'm going to get read. The other thing by having a physical newsletter is people, they don't just usually dump them. They keep them around for a few weeks. They didn't get to read at all. When you train people, this is really interesting. I just figured this out recently. I was talking to somebody who was advertising in different media. We've been advertising in magazines. I was talking about the difference between the response we get with some email blasts we're getting versus some magazines we're getting. They were telling me the kind of leads you're getting from a daily newspaper. It's really interesting that the frequency of the media form which you're using is actually what trains people how quickly to respond. They find an offer in today's newspaper that they like. They're probably going to respond today because they know they're getting another paper tomorrow. The offer is no longer on the table. Right. That's really interesting. The rate at which you send a media to your clients is the rate at which they get trained to hold them. If there's a newsletter that comes once a month, they know I don't need to get rid of this today. I can put it aside. They're not just going to chuck it because they're worried about having to consume it today. I end up in the bathroom and I'm like, who knows where it ends up? Totally. It's there. The other thing that we do in our newsletters is we three hole punch them. If something has three holes in it, it means it's supposed to be put in a bio. I've got to go keep it somewhere. I've got to put it somewhere. You can do tricks like that to add value to it. We send all of our newsletters now in a clear envelope, which is cool. It allows people to see what it is and know what it is. Another mistake a lot of people make when doing newsletter, there are times when you want it to be a full-color glossy magazine and there are times when you don't. The biggest fear you should have is showing up like just another rag in the mail. Another coupon clip or whatever it is, you do not want to show up like that. For a long time and for certain things like for our law firm, we use black 9x12 linen envelopes with silver foil. We scare the hell out of people so we have to be careful how we use them. You get a big black envelope from law firm. It does get open. But there are many different styles you can use to start getting open. It really is all about creating different types of media to show up in your clients' world, whatever that one is. 100%. A couple things I like to talk about with that is we don't ask for more email. I don't need any more. Don't send me anything else. Our mailbox is shrinking. There might only be four or five, six things in there and they're either usually bills or just that regular correspondence. When you show up in a 9x12 envelope with your face on it, it's like, wow, here's something cool of value that isn't a bill, that isn't a val pack, that isn't the flyer or whatever it is on the weekdays. And the rate at which postage goes up, the rate at which everyone else starts to drop. Your competition is not going to spend the money. We talked about it earlier. Are you willing and able to spend the most money to acquire a customer but then keep that customer because there's a ton of money in a whole different subject in repeat business and reactivating people, getting them reengaged. I mean, if someone does a bestselling book with us, that person that then turns into a TV person, they're the best client in the entire world. Right. They didn't have to spend any more money. It's a great point to talk about right now. There's only only three ways to grow your business. Right. To go out and get new customers, to sell your existing customers more dollars worth of stuff or to sell them more frequently. Right? Yeah. And getting new customers is the most expensive. We don't have to do it. Right. But most people have a big hole in the back of their ship that they're bringing people in and they're falling right out the back. Yeah. And so that's just a key point, I think, just no matter what we're talking about. So let's turn some of this stuff. Right? So now we have our market. We have our story and now we're showing up like a celebrity. Right? So let's talk about how we translate that into money. And in the book, you and Jack talked about kind of this ladder of ascension. Right? And this is, some people understand the concept, but they don't really know how to put it into play. Like I'm a dentist. I don't have an ascension funnel. Right? I'm not an info marketer or something. So let's talk about what this ladder of ascension is and how we can take everything we've been talking about and stick it into. Sure. So the basic concept for those of you who don't know or haven't heard that term, the ladder of ascension, it's a Dan Kennedy term again. Yeah. It is the fact of taking someone and giving them something with low to no barrier, whether it's free or inexpensive, he uses a newsletter, other things, and then ultimately get them in the door for $100,000, copyrighting, consulting or whatever that he does. Right. But there's all these places along the way that you want people to be able to rise to the level at which they're ready to and that you want them to and spend more money with you. Right? Because most people won't go zero to a hundred grand. Right? Mostly won't go zero to a grand without something in between. Right? And we also talked about the fact, you know, I've talked a lot about the fact that if you don't have the steps in between zero to a hundred grand, you almost can't justify the hundred grand. Right. Because it's like, well, there's free and then there's a hundred grand. Well, why is it a hundred grand? Well, if I had, you know, if I know that, you know, just an information product with you is $10,000 and it's an hour-long information project and I get to spend three days with you for a hundred grand, well, now I can kind of figure out why the value would be there. So a lot of people miss that image. I missed it for years not having the things in between. I didn't particularly care about having products because I didn't mind teaching people things, but I just didn't, you know, it was more valuable to me to have somebody in for a day and or to work on their business privately. But the fact of the matter is, like the product you're watching right now, build a position for the fact, it's, I'm giving you knowledge. I'm going to gain trust. You're going to see that we have value, all those things, which are great, but it's going to build a position on why, well, I paid several thousand dollars for this product. Well, then I understand why I would have to spend six, seven, ten thousand dollars to spend a couple of days at an event and understand the value I'm going to get. So the, the very simply put, the ascension ladder is just low to no barrier and cost at the bottom to your top level at the top. And so for our business, let's walk through that. So we do free podcasts, we do video podcasts. We do, I do a lot of tele seminars and webinars. I speak at a lot of other people's events. People have typically paid to go, but I'm not typically getting paid to speak. I'm going there on my own accord because I know the business can come out of it. But so low to no barrier. Free reports, free books, things like that as well. Or even a $20 book at Amazon or whatever. Low to no barrier. We have an offer sometimes where it's a low fee to get our book shipped to you out of magazines and stuff. Right. Again, that's, that's, that's the, it's an irresistible, there's no brainer why I wouldn't want to get that content, you know, for free to low, low price point. Exactly. So that's what you want to try to create. And that's, that's a start of a lead funnel, right? Right. That's where you're trying to get people who you introduce them to you and, and try to get them to, to take some action. Ideas that involves a credit card, no matter how low the amount, the much more engaged they will be. Yeah. But so you start there and then you start coming into our world, you start getting emails and newsletters and all that sort of stuff. And you start getting postcards and brochures and getting invited on a tele-signal hours. And in the podcast every week, we do a break kind of and we, and we offer some sort of opportunity that we have going. And so then, you know, you might become a best selling author with us in our program for about whatever it is, $497 a month. And then, but you might rather get the $1,097 a month program. I'm just making up numbers here because who knows what they'll be when you watch this. But TV show, be on our TV show and be in a book and then you're going to come to our bestseller summit. And then from there, you might want to get into our X group, which is kind of our mastermind and network group. And then you might want to have a private consulting day. You might, you know, we have people who hire us, we have people who buy our books for $20 and we have people who hire us for over $100,000 a year to do all sorts of stuff. By the time we pay that kind of money, I mean, we're practically running a lot of stuff for you. We get afraid, but we have a level for everybody who wants something different. Like my documentaries is we do on people, right? That's a higher price point than collaborating on a book with someone else, right? But we give people those points of entry and we give them somewhere to go. Right. Well, too, so it gives them somewhere to go, right? So people always want, you had a client you recently worked with, they're like, you know, Nick, I'm a premium package kind of guy, right? So you always have people that want this level, right? Whether it's ego or whatever the case is, but they always want more, but most people aren't ready for that level. So they got to work their way up to that level. Going back to Dan Kennedy, everyone aspires to be in his platinum group, which is his platinum mastermind group, but day one, you're not ready for it. Right. You're just going through the basic concepts. Again, it could be ego, but most of the time is like, they're ready for that now. Right. Like that, oh, now I'm finally ready for this. I can do this. Yeah, when we first started following Dan's stuff, I couldn't have hoped to pay to be in his platinum group. The business just didn't have enough money to do it. Right. So it's like $30 something thousand dollars a year. But now the leverage we get from being in there and the business we get out of it and the problem with Dan, it makes sense for the business of our size, right? Exactly. And so that's what the ladder of ascension is. It's ultimately not having two rungs and people have to jump off because there's nowhere else to go. Right. So let's talk about maybe how this ladder of ascension will work in some more traditional businesses, right? So I'm a dentist. So what does my ladder of ascension look like in a dental practice? Sure. So what you first might offer is maybe a free report, a special report on the top 10 ways to have pain-free dental care for your kids, whatever you're specializing in. Right. And it goes back to the niche. So serve the niche, find the problem in the niche, and then create this reporter book or video. And address it, right? So that, again, this is now a value to the prospect. It's not just a business card or a flyer. This is something that has value. There's education and knowledge in here. One of the things we also talk to our clients about a lot is if you can teach your prospect something they didn't know, they didn't know, you get the trust. Because they've been looking at this stuff for years or talking to three or four different people and no one told them about this. So what else have they been withholding that's important? So if you reveal the stuff, it's like a few years ago there was a beer commercial and they talked about how these beer bottles are cold filtered and blah, blah. And as it came, everyone was like enamored with this beer. And it turns out everyone does this. Because no one was talking about the fact that they do this. So they built in this separation now because they started talking about it. So it was allowing that. So we now have this, they talked to something they didn't know, they didn't know. And they built trust. So now we've got the special report. Okay, so then you might invite people from the special report. You might invite them onto a Tel Aviv webinar. It might be free. It might be or a group event in your office where we're going to go through, I don't know how to get cosmetics that make you look and feel great and make more money. I don't know what it is. But that might be a low charge or there may be no charge for it. It could also be a free consultation or something as well. Free consultation. Now you're starting to build some trust. Then it may be, after the consultation, it may be a exploratory visit, whatever it is. That might be $97. And then ultimately it may end up with, well, there's two choices. You've got the $500 tooth whitening package or the $5,000, let me change out some of your teeth. I don't know. But there's a way to use it. Through all this, the people who are good prospects, sometimes hard to tell so you end up sending more than you need to. But you're sending them newsletters, sending them postcards. You're creating that relationship and you're giving them constant opportunities, again, by Tel Aviv webinar, to get educated, to learn. You've got to figure out what the life cycle is of this sale. Ultimately, you want to keep educating them too. The more you keep in touch with them, the more they're going to send you and one of their friends. Everyone loves referrals. There's something easiest business to get. But it's all by continuing this communication cycle. We even have a dentist who was in our mastermind at one point. She was an orthodontist. She said, well, this is the thing. People come to me, they get their stuff, and then they're down the other teeth fixing, kind of down. I was like, well, are there typically either retainers? Oh, yeah. Oftentimes there's a retainer that they're supposed to wear. But typically they'll lose those and they'll never come back. We helped her start offering this retainer insurance program where you got some number, there wasn't unlimited. But they're paying some number, people who are never typically coming back. We're now spending $29, $39, whatever dollars a month or a year, whatever it was. It was essentially free money. She had to replace some retainers, but the numbers way worked out. But because she had a place for them to go, they'd come through her highest level service. And literally there's nothing more she could do for them. Their teeth were straight. They're great. She did her job well, but there's still an opportunity to add and add on so that they could do that. So just interesting. I love that. So let's talk a little bit about, so now we have this ladder. And hopefully in your business, you're creating your ladder and there's an exercise where you can develop each of these different levels from that low free barrier all the way to your top level, your premium level. But now let's talk about, so I get the free thing to get to the next thing. You talked a little bit about educating them, but it's also again being seen as the celebrity expert so they can't go anywhere else because most people just get into straight sales mode. Like, hey, you're interested in this. Why don't you buy my 9999 widget and do this, this, and that. They turn into used car salesman, no offense to used car salesman, but that's what they turn into immediately. That's not what a celebrity does. You never see Brad Pitt walk in his way, you got to go see my new movie. He's only 799. He just buy one, get one for each. He wouldn't do that. He sends you previews and he's doing cameos. He's doing interviews, but he makes sure you see all of that. So let's talk about the difference between a salesperson and an expert and how we can use media to be positioned as a celebrity as they go through this funnel. Absolutely. I'm going to answer that in one second, but you made a point I don't want to forget. The premium service that you have, whether you offer it now or not, make something up, the ultimate, the thing that would make you so excited to deliver this and get paid this amount of money. Because whatever your premium service is, someone will buy it number one. And number two, it makes your middle ground service look like the obvious choice because people typically don't want to be on the cheap end. They don't typically want to be on the most expensive end. Some do, but a lot will fall in the middle. So whatever your premium is now, make that your middle. And you'll have a lot more people funnel into your current premium end. You just kind of keep moving that ladder higher and higher. Okay, so we're talking about, what we're talking about. Most people versus experts and how to look like a celebrity through this process. So we all know, we all hate being sold. We love to buy things, but we hate being sold. And we know how salespeople show up. We know when we walk into the shoe store at the mall, we know what the salesman looks like. When we walk into most car dealerships, we get a lot of great car dealerships that do not do things this way. They all fight and beeline for you. You know what the salesman looks like. When the water purification guy comes to your door, you know what the salesman looks like. He's got his briefcase with his PowerPoint thing in it. He's showing up like a salesman. And one of the biggest mistakes a lot of people make when building their position, he's showing up like a salesman. And so not there's anything wrong with selling. Selling is amazing. It's how the world goes round, right? But selling is not evil. No, selling is amazing. But you don't want to look like a salesman because people will shut off. One of the things that we have to tell a lot of our clients about is, look, you probably don't want to put all of your sales awards and stuff in your bio because then people just know you're a good salesman. It doesn't mean that you got to make sure. It means, wow, I'm in the room with this guy. He's going to sell me something. So let me turn off. It's like going to like a timeshare presentation. Yes. Even my wife and I, we went in and we're like, do you want to buy a timeshare? No. Do you want to buy a timeshare? No. We prep ourselves to not buy when we know we're going to get sold. And the guy's got top sales awards for November to December. You want to talk to the other guy. Yeah, you are scared that you put your wallet back in the car. We don't want that to happen with your clients prospects. But what we do want you to use is things, not that awards are bad. It just depends on how their position. We get clients awards that position way better. But you want to show up like a consultant, right? Right. As a consultant show, he doesn't show up with a pre-filled out sales thing. Right. He shows up with a blank pad of paper. Right. Now he knows exactly what he's going to write. A well-scripted salesman. He's scripted, but you can't tell. Right. He's got a bag of tricks that he's ready to put in. Absolutely. Right. But the fact of the matter is he shows up differently, looks differently. So, okay, so as a PR firm, that's interesting. So we're a media market PR firm. How do we show up? Well, by the way, most people who are media market PR firms haven't written their own books. They haven't been in major media. Themselves. Yeah. So we do those sorts of things. There are different types of firms around the world that do pieces of what we do. I've never seen anyone who does it all. And it would be so hard to find someone who would do it all because we have our own language in a lot of ways. Right. And that's an important thing too. You want to start creating terms around what you do. We own the trademark on celebrity branding, celebrity expert. We have so many trademarks because the secret formula for media success, the business trifecta, because we want to explain things to you that are common sense, but maybe not common knowledge. But now you're even using our language with them. I don't think I tell if someone is ripping me off, another competitor in the marketplace because they start using my terms like, well, they've been watching my stuff. But owning that language is important. But then when you start talking in my language, you go to another media market PR firm and ask them what they think about the business trifecta and they have a blank stare. They haven't talked to anything. You don't have zero trust for them. You're probably not going to hire them. So you've got to do that. But you want to show up with the way we show up is like with our best selling books, with our media placements in like we have a feature that was in the USA Today. Right. We have a story. We put that in there. We have the movie about our firm. So we start showing up in the types of media people are used to seeing celebrities. And those media formats allow us to tell our story too. Yeah, I completely agree. And the biggest thing is you want to use this stuff. Right. And online too. I don't want to forget about online. Online is great. Online drives a lot for us because again, people are looking for us. They go to our website. They want to check out more. They want to see. Totally. Right. And so online blogs, articles, press release, that's again more content that we can see. When people go to our website, they couldn't, well, they could possibly take a long time to read all of our blogs and stuff, but most people wouldn't. But just the fact that they can scroll for days, they know that you have voluminous amounts of content and you must be next. Yeah, exactly. So the goal here in to become this celebrity expert is to use, find that niche, find that marketplace, develop your story, get that major media credibility and now insert it into your marketing funnel, whether it's online or offline. So when you send a postcard out, it says, hey, this is, you know, Nick Nanton, Emmy award-winning director and best-selling author, use that language in your pieces and then continue to follow up. Right. Again, a celebrity doesn't just have one preview for a movie and that's it. Right. They're doing previews in every single commercial and then they do a tie-in with someone else. And so you need to do that same thing. So you need to set up different funnels, different systems, whatever you want to call it, using all of these different media pieces. Right. So we talked about blogs. We have, you know, hundreds of blog articles, but we use them in a follow-up sequence. So someone's interested and engaged with us. We'll send them a certain number of blog posts that are about the topic that they wanted to know about, right? So they get the special report and then maybe they do business with us. Maybe they don't. If they don't, then we send them a blog post and another blog post or an article that you wrote on Fast Company or a tear sheet or a copy of the book. And so we're using this media in our marketing, which goes back to the secret formula for media success, using that major media in your direct media. Right. Every time we create a new piece of media, it's really to fill a hole in the marketing funnel. Right. We do collaborative books a lot. We do a lot of them. And we put ourselves in all the ones that are appropriate. Like I'm not in the dental book and some of those things. But we put ourselves in those and we really have to come up with other ways to talk about the same things that we do. So what do we do? We think about what's really hot right now, what are the questions people are asking. And then we write a chapter on it, put it in the book, make that a bestseller. Now when that question comes up again, oh, great, here's a copy of my book. I wrote this chapter. There's lots of other great experts in here too. But we wrote this chapter about exactly what you're asking about. Before we were featured in a bestselling book and we took that chapter and broke it out in a special report because we always get asked this question, check it out. So now you have systemized ways to answer questions and they have much more authority when it came out of a bestselling book or it's a special report or whatever. Right. They get that. So we do that all the time and we create media to answer the questions we keep getting asked. I love that. So as we wrap up here in this third stage in developing your celebrity brand, here are some exercises that I want you to go through and they are in your action guide. So make sure you have that in front of you as you go through it. The first thing I want you to do is to create a media and asset inventory. Right. So what are the blog posts that you have that answer those questions? What are the special reports that you have? What are the videos? You know, a lot of people, they have all these YouTube videos, but they don't use them. They're just out there, you know, hoping that people find them. Now it's up to you to insert them. So create an inventory. And so there's an inventory checklist for you to create in your action guide. The second piece is to look at all the first impressionable places that you are. That might have not been the right English to use, but all the places where people can find you for the first time and really think about what they see. Right. So if they come in and they come to your website first, does that website show that you're a celebrity expert? We've actually created a bonus module with our expert on the online area, Lindsay Dix, where she helps you to develop that celebrity website, that marketing website. But if the website's the point of entry, does it show that you're a best-selling author? Does it show your media credentials? Or does it look like you just slapped up a template from the 90s? Right. If it's you speaking, right? Are you dressed appropriately for that? If it's a special report, if it's a book going back to your story, you know, about when you first started developing Sleby Branding U, is it a cover that you got for $5 on Fiverr? Or is it someone that really makes your art and your work look great? So the first impression is very, very key. And then the third step is this prospect funnel. What are the things that you want your marketplace to know about you before they hire you? I think this is really key in our publishing business. We have a free report on how to write a book and grow your business empire. And we also have the nine publishing myths report that we give to them. Then we have some tear sheets. We have blog posts. We have an agent that calls them. And we've developed a funnel that answers all the objections that they would have. And so we have that for the books, and we have that for TV, and we have that for big print, and we have that for films, and we have that for web. And so look at all of those different funnels. And now take this media inventory, this asset inventory, take those first impressions, and now build the funnel with the stuff. Right? Hopefully that made sense. It made a lot of sense. And that's exactly what you need to do. And we'll see you in the next module. We'll see you in the next one. Thanks, guys.
